question: What miracle does Elder Holland use as the anchor for his talk 'And Now I See'?
answer: The healing of the man born blind in John 9, where Jesus made clay from spit and dirt, applied it to the man's eyes, and told him to wash in the pool of Siloam.

feedback.text: See John 9:1–7. Elder Holland uses this miracle to illustrate that God can bless us through any method He chooses, no matter how plain or unexpected the instrument.

question: What was the healed blind man's simple testimony to the Pharisees who questioned him?
answer: "Whether he be a sinner … I know not: but one thing I do know, … whereas I was blind, now I see." (John 9:25)

feedback.text: Elder Holland uses this as the model of evidence-based testimony — personal, irreducible, and immune to argument. You cannot argue someone out of what they have genuinely experienced.

question: According to Elder Holland, why did Jesus's enemies confront the healed man after his miracle?
options: They were curious how the healing happened
They feared the miracle would add to the threat Jesus posed to their authority
They wanted to verify the man had truly been blind
They hoped the man would deny Jesus performed the miracle
answer: They feared the miracle would increase Jesus's influence and threaten their presumed authority over the people.

feedback.text: Holland notes that opposition to truth often comes not from honest inquiry but from those whose power or position feels threatened by the evidence.

question: Which of the following does Elder Holland NOT mention as an example of God using a humble or unexpected instrument?
options: A baby lying in a manger
An unlearned boy on a New York farm
A newly called Relief Society president
A fisherman casting his net on the sea
answer: Holland mentions a baby in a manger, Joseph Smith (an unlearned boy on a NY farm), a newly called Relief Society president, and a brand-new missionary — but not a fisherman.

feedback.text: The pattern Holland highlights: God consistently chooses instruments the world would overlook. This connects to Isaiah 53:2 — 'there is no beauty that we should desire him.'

question: What does Elder Holland say is his personal 'sight-giving, life-giving encounter with real evidence of truth'?
answer: The Book of Mormon: Another Testament of Jesus Christ. He calls it 'a rod of safety for my soul, a transcendent and penetrating light of revelation.'

feedback.text: Holland draws a direct parallel: just as spit and dirt opened the blind man's eyes, the Book of Mormon opened his spiritual eyes — dismissed by critics for the same reason the clay seemed embarrassing.

question: Elder Holland claims that priesthood authority in the Church is granted on the basis of impressive academic credentials or theological training.
answer: FALSE. Holland states that priesthood authority 'is not granted on the basis of impressive credentials nor determined by tradition or birthright … [it is] conferred only by the laying on of hands' in an unbroken line back to Jesus Christ.

feedback.text: This connects to the Abrahamic priesthood — Abraham sought and received priesthood authority through 'the fathers' (Abraham 1:2–4), not through the idolatrous tradition of his own father.

question: What does Elder Holland say is unique evidence of God's truth that is not found elsewhere in the world — only in The Church of Jesus Christ of Latter-day Saints?
answer: Temple work for deceased family members — vicarious ordinances performed on behalf of the dead, demonstrating God's universal love for both the living and the dead.

feedback.text: This connects to the Abrahamic covenant — 'as many as receive this Gospel shall be accounted thy seed' (Abraham 2:10–11). The covenant extends across generations and beyond death.

question: What was Abraham's central desire as stated in Abraham 1:2?
answer: "To be a greater follower of righteousness" and to receive the blessings of the fathers, including the right to administer the priesthood.

feedback.text: The Come, Follow Me guide emphasizes that God honored Abraham's desire even though his own father had abandoned the true worship of God. Desire + action = God's support.

question: According to the Come, Follow Me guide, how does the Abrahamic covenant apply to people today who are baptized members of the Church?
options: They inherit the covenant only if they are biological descendants of Abraham
They are adopted into Abraham's family and accounted as his seed
They receive a separate covenant unrelated to Abraham's
The covenant only applies to those who serve missions
answer: Members are adopted into Abraham's family through the gospel. Abraham 2:10–11 states 'as many as receive this Gospel shall be … accounted thy seed.' Galatians 3:26–29 and D&C 132:30–32 confirm this.

feedback.text: This is why Holland's point about temple work for the dead is so powerful — God's covenant is not limited by time, birth, or geography. It extends to all who receive the gospel, including those who never had the chance in mortality.

question: Abraham came from a supportive, righteous family environment that helped prepare him to receive the Abrahamic covenant.
answer: FALSE. Abraham's own father had abandoned true worship of God and even tried to have Abraham sacrificed to false gods. The CFM guide notes this as evidence that family history does not determine one's future with God.

feedback.text: This parallels Holland's theme: the instrument (Abraham's family background) was broken and unpromising. Yet God honored Abraham's desire. The future can be filled with hope regardless of family history.

question: In the Come, Follow Me reading, 'Ishmael' is a Hebrew name. What does it mean, and why is that significant in Genesis 16?
options: God provides — Hagar was promised food and water
God hears — Hagar felt alone and mistreated, yet God heard her
God saves — Ishmael was rescued from death in the wilderness
God sees — Hagar saw an angel of the Lord
answer: Ishmael means 'God hears.' Hagar felt abandoned and mistreated, yet the naming of her son proclaimed that God had heard her distress — even when she was an outsider.

feedback.text: The CFM guide pairs this with Holland's central theme: God sees and hears those the world overlooks. He used plain means — a name, a spring of water, a messenger — to bless Hagar just as He used spit and clay to heal the blind man.

question: What two Old Testament examples does Elder Holland cite of people who rejected God's method of blessing because the means seemed too plain or humble?
answer: 1) Naaman, who resisted washing in the River Jordan to be healed of leprosy (2 Kings 5). 2) The children of Israel who refused to look at the serpent on the staff to be healed from snake bites (Numbers 21).

feedback.text: Both examples appear in Holland's talk. The Come, Follow Me guide also references these patterns in the context of Abraham — will we accept God's blessings through His chosen means, or will we demand something more impressive?

question: How does Elder Holland describe what the Book of Mormon has been for him personally?
options: A historical record that proves the Church is true
A rod of safety, a light of revelation, and an illumination of the path through darkness
A collection of prophecies about the latter days
A guide to priesthood authority and ordinances
answer: Holland calls it 'a rod of safety for my soul, a transcendent and penetrating light of revelation, an illumination of the path I must walk when mists of darkness come.'

feedback.text: Notice the imagery: a rod (like Moses's staff, like the iron rod), light (contrasted with blindness and darkness), and path (covenant journey). All of these connect to the themes of sight restored that run through the talk.

question: According to Abraham 2:6–11, God asked Abraham to receive blessings only — Abraham had no obligation to bless others.
answer: FALSE. God told Abraham to 'be a blessing' (Genesis 12:2). Abraham 2:11 states that through Abraham's seed, 'all the families of the earth [should] be blessed.' The covenant was both receiving and extending blessings.

feedback.text: The CFM guide emphasizes this: 'Besides promising blessings, God told Abraham to be a blessing.' This is the covenant obligation — to extend what we have received to others, which is exactly the purpose of missionary work and temple work.

question: What hymn does Elder Holland quote at the close of 'And Now I See,' and why is it fitting?
answer: 'Amazing Grace.' The hymn's language — 'I once was lost, but now am found, was blind, but now I see' — mirrors the blind man's testimony and the entire theme of spiritual sight restored through Christ.

feedback.text: Holland frames himself as the newly healed man: old enough to testify, having been near death, having experienced the Holy Spirit. His testimony is simple and personal — the same evidence the blind man offered.
